era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Egypt; the Old Kingdom of Egypt is in its later phases (Dynasty V–VI transition period). In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic III period is underway, with city-states like Lagash, Umma, and Uruk competing for regional dominance. The Indus Valley Civilisation is in its early mature phase. Writing systems (cuneiform, hieroglyphics) are well-established in river valleys; most of the world remains preliterate.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (no written records; archaeological record thin)
Northern Europe and the Steppe (sparse archaeological evidence)
East Asia beyond the Yellow River valley (limited contemporary documentation)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Pepi II (Neferkare) of Egypt — Old Kingdom Dynasty VI
events.description: Pepi II is traditionally dated to have reigned during this period, though his exact chronology remains debated. He is credited with an exceptionally long reign (possibly 64 years in some king lists, though modern scholars debate this). His reign is associated with continued centralised pharaonic authority, though some evidence suggests weakening of royal power toward the end of the Old Kingdom. Expeditions to Nubia and the Red Sea are attested in inscriptions. His reign marks a transitional period before the collapse of centralised authority in the First Intermediate Period.

sources.name: Abydos King List (Temple of Seti I)
Autobiographical inscriptions from officials' tombs (e.g., Harkhuf, Sabni)

events.certainty_note: Pepi II's reign is attested in multiple king lists and official inscriptions, but absolute dating is uncertain. Conventional chronology places him c. 2278–2184 BCE; the year -3090 (3091 BCE) is approximate. Egyptological consensus on his reign length and exact dates varies; some scholars compress his reign significantly.
cross_references: Follows from -3091 (reign of Pepi I or Merenre)

events.title: Lagash–Umma conflict in Mesopotamia — Early Dynastic III
events.region: Mesopotamia (southern Iraq, Lagash and Umma city-states)
events.description: The city-states of Lagash and Umma engaged in a long-running territorial dispute over the Gu-edena (border region). Multiple military campaigns are attested in inscriptions from both cities. Eannatum of Lagash is credited with victories over Umma and other rivals, though the exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain. These conflicts are among the earliest documented wars in human history, recorded in contemporary inscriptions and later king lists. The disputes reflect competition for agricultural land and water rights in the Tigris-Euphrates delta.
key_figures: Ush of Umma — Ensi of Umma

sources.name: Stele of Eannatum (Lagash, c. 2450 BCE conventional dating)
Inscriptions of Entemena of Lagash (successor to Eannatum)

events.certainty_note: The Lagash–Umma conflicts are well-attested in contemporary inscriptions, but absolute dating is uncertain. Conventional chronology places Eannatum c. 2450 BCE; the assignment to -3090 is approximate and depends on which chronological system is used (short, middle, or long chronology). The year -3090 may or may not correspond to an active phase of these conflicts.
cross_references: Part of broader Early Dynastic III period of Mesopotamian city-state competition
Precedes the rise of the Akkadian Empire (c. 2334 BCE conventional)

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Pepi II's reign and the Lagash–Umma conflicts is highly uncertain. Egyptological chronology for the Old Kingdom varies by ±100–200 years depending on the system used (short, middle, or long chronology). Some scholars argue that Pepi II's reign was much shorter than the 64 years attributed to him in some king lists, possibly only 30–40 years. The assignment of these events to the year -3090 is therefore approximate and contingent on chronological assumptions. Additionally, the 'long reign' of Pepi II may be a historiographic exaggeration or conflation of multiple rulers.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this period is heavily dependent on king lists compiled centuries or millennia after the events, supplemented by contemporary inscriptions from tombs and temples. Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom is notoriously uncertain; different scholarly systems (short, middle, long) can shift dates by 100+ years. Mesopotamian chronology is similarly debated. The surviving sources are biased toward elite, literate cultures (Egypt, Mesopotamia, Indus Valley); vast regions of the world are archaeologically invisible. Inscriptions tend to emphasise royal achievements and military victories, potentially exaggerating their significance. The year -3090 itself is a conventional assignment; the actual events may have occurred several decades earlier or later.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's reign ends; the Old Kingdom collapses into the First Intermediate Period
Lagash–Umma conflicts continue into the following period
